# GSoC 2026 Proposal: Fine-tuning AI transcription models for Greek Municipal Councils

## Basic Details

* **Full Name**: Angelos Papamichail  
* **Email**: angelos.papamichail@gmail.com  
* **GitHub Username**: [angelospk](https://github.com/angelospk)  
* **First Language**: Greek  
* **Location and Timezone**: Thessaloniki, Greece (UTC+2)  
* **Previous Work on Open Source**:  
  * [OpenCouncil](https://github.com/schemalabz/opencouncil): Making local government more transparent and accessible.  
* **Other Projects**:  
  * [Emotion-Recognition-Addon](https://github.com/beyondTheMyth/Emotion-Recognition-Addon): Python/ML project for real-time sentiment analysis using SVMs.  
  * [SubtitleKit](https://github.com/angelospk/SubtitleKit): AI-powered tool for subtitle processing and filler word removal.

## Motivation

**What is your motivation to take part in Google Summer of Code?**  
I want to work on a real ML pipeline with people who'll give me honest feedback. Most of my project experience so far has been academic or personal experimentation. GSoC is a way to close that gap on something that actually delivers.

**Why did you choose GFOSS?**  
OpenCouncil is what sold me. It's civic infrastructure, not a flashy product, but something that quietly determines whether municipal decisions are accessible to the public. GFOSS takes that kind of work seriously, and I wanted to be around that.

**Why do you want to work on this particular project?**  
 I have sat through enough Greek council recordings to know the transcription failures aren't random. Regional accents, overlapping speakers, and legal terminology that Whisper has clearly never seen, the errors follow patterns. I've been contributing to OpenCouncil for a while and did an initial analysis on 5,000 correction pairs from the database. My read is that fine-tuning Whisper on this data, with LLM post-correction, is the right fix. I want to find out if I'm right.

I have an MEng in ECE from AUTH, software engineering track, so the ML pipeline work is familiar ground. The Greek civic speech problem is specific enough that I think I can actually move the needle.

**What are your expectations from us during and after the successful completion of the program?**  
Mostly: push back when my implementation decisions are wrong. I'll be making real tradeoffs on training data, quantization strategy, and eval methodology, and I'd rather have a mentor who disagrees with me than one who approves everything. After GSoC, I intend to stay on as a maintainer. The model should be usable by other Greek NLP projects, not just OpenCouncil.

## Current Status

The diagram below shows the OpenCouncil task server's processing flow. This project is focused on the transcription stages; the current setup hits external APIs, and the goal is to replace that with a fine-tuned open-source model that handles Greek council speech better. The downstream error correction phase is of secondary interest.

Figure 1: Diagram of Council Meeting Processing to extract its transcript 

The architecture isn't novel. [SuperWhisper](https://superwhisper.com/) and [Wispr Flow](https://wisprflow.ai/) already use Whisper-to-LLM post-correction in production. ASR gets you a strong first draft; the language model cleans it up. This project applies that same pipeline to a domain where it hasn't been done properly yet.

### Correction Analysis Insights

After consulting with my mentors, I [analyzed 5,000 human-verified transcription correction pairs](#appendix-a:-corrections’-sample-analysis-and-error-taxonomy) drawn from real municipal council proceedings; reverberant chambers, overlapping speakers, heavy administrative jargon. Three error categories account for everything:

* **Acoustic/Phonetic & Proper Noun Errors (\~42%):** Greek-specific confusions like iotacism (e.g., πτέσματα instead of πταίσματα) and consonant cluster errors (e.g., χτήματα vs. κτήματα). Proper noun failures are their own problem; roughly 11% of total errors, local surnames and municipal place names that don't appear in Whisper's default vocabulary at all. These come from acoustic misrecognition, which makes them the main target for fine-tuning.  
* **Morphological Errors (\~33%):** Root morpheme errors like καταδαφιστεί instead of κατεδαφιστεί. Analysis points to the acoustic model's internal language model bias as the cause, not actual mispronunciation. Fine-tuning should catch most of these before they reach the LLM.  
* **Linguistic, Formatting & Acronym Errors (\~25%):** More than 1,200 cases: punctuation and capitalization omissions, grammatical agreement errors (e.g., τις παρόντες instead of τους παρόντες), mangled administrative acronyms (e.g., ΣΥΔΟΜ instead of ΥΔΟΜ), and tokenizer artifacts; the ASCII "?" turning up where the Greek semicolon ";" should be.

### Related Work (Greek ASR Fine Tuning)

Fine-tuning smaller models on domain data reliably beats bigger general models used zero-shot. For Greek, fine-tuning smaller Whisper variants on the Greek Podcast Corpus cut WER by half compared to zero-shot Whisper-large-v2 [(Paraskevopoulos et al, 2024\)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.15284). And you don't need to retrain every parameter: LoRA matched full fine-tuning performance for Greek medical dictation at a fraction of the compute [(Georgilas & Stafylakis, 2025\)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.23550).

## Proposal Details

**What are you making?**

The proposal is focused on acoustic optimization in OpenCouncil's primary transcription stage. The plan is to fine-tune a Whisper ASR model on human-corrected Greek municipal recordings using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation).

Fine-tuning targets the problems the current model handles worst: Greek iotacism, consonant-cluster errors, and morphological root mistakes. The practical goal is a lower first-pass Word Error Rate, specifically by making the model more reliable on administrative terminology and proper nouns, which is where it currently drops the ball most often.

**![](data:image/png;base64,)**
Figure 2: Diagram Representation of Whisper Fine Tuning

**What technologies will you be using?**

* **Models**: [OpenAI Whisper Large (v2 or v3)](https://huggingface.co/openai/whisper-large-v3)**.** I'll start from Whisper-large-v3 as the base ([best zero shot Greek results](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.15284): 11-17% WER depending on domain) and also try large-v3-turbo as a lighter option, since a LoRA-adapted smaller model might get close to large-v3 quality for much less compute. The new [Cohere transcribe model](https://www.google.com/url?source=gmail&sa=E&q=https://huggingface.co/CohereLabs/cohere-transcribe-03-2026) will also be included for comparative testing.  
* **Training**: [PyTorch](https://pytorch.org/) ≥ 2.3, [HuggingFace Transformers](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers) ≥ 4.40, [PEFT](https://github.com/huggingface/peft) (LoRA \+ 8-bit quantization via [bitsandbytes](https://github.com/TimDettmers/bitsandbytes)).  
* **Evaluation**: [jiwer](https://github.com/jitsi/jiwer) for WER/CER with Greek NFKC normalization.  
* **Data**: Database extracted human-corrected correction pairs from OpenCouncil (over 335h of usable training data with corrections’ data from 309 meetings across 18 municipalities). Could also be used: [HParl corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.12883) (Greek Parliament proceedings dataset from ILSP); [Greek Podcast Corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.15284) (\~800h of weakly labeled speech); [Common Voice Greek](https://datacollective.mozillafoundation.org/datasets/cmn2cx91x01dno10754vxfu3b).  
* **Tooling**: Python, Next.js/TypeScript (OpenCouncil pipeline integration).

### LoRA Fine-Tuning Configuration

Full fine-tuning requires 40+ GB VRAM and is impractical for this project. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) trains only 2–3% of parameters, produces \~50 MB adapter files, and achieves comparable WER to full fine-tuning.

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **LoRA rank (r)** | 32 | Starting point: will sweep r ∈ {8, 16, 32} during Phase 2 to find the best quality/VRAM tradeoff. |
| **LoRA alpha** | 64 | 2× rank is the standard |
| **Target modules** | q, k, v, out, fc1, fc2 | All attention \+ FFN projections |
| **Learning rate** | 1e-3 | Standard for LoRA adapters. [Paraskevopoulos et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.15284) used 1e-5 for full fine tuning; LoRA typically needs a higher learning rate |
| **Effective batch size** | 16 | 4 per device × 4 accumulation |
| **Max steps** | 1000 | LoRA converges faster |
| **Quantization & VRAM** | 8-bit (BnB) on 24GB GPU | Accommodates long-form audio activations & prevents OOM |

## Architecture Integration & Implementation Plan

src/tasks/transcribe.ts is hardwired to Gladia via GladiaTranscriber. I'll add a WhisperTranscriber that takes the same TranscribeArgs → Transcript shape from src/types.ts. createPipeline() already does dependency injection, so nothing downstream needs touching.  
The fine-tuned model also gives per-utterance confidence scores. fixTranscript.ts can use these to skip LLM calls on high-confidence segments — fewer unnecessary corrections, lower API spend.

[Based on the literature](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qMP2H4xGiC179SEL7wrDdH0onEv0v4rIUn4KSGU6NR4/edit?pli=1&tab=t.0#heading=h.nb4xlcct9mww), I plan to explore training on concatenated segments. Fine-tuning Whisper on short utterances (\~2.3s average) wrecks long-form performance — timestamps drift, segmentation falls apart. Fix: concatenate sequential utterances via `startTimestamp`/`endTimestamp` into synthetic 2–5 minute segments, with silence gaps intact. The model then still handles full-length council recordings at inference.

### Evaluation Methodology

### Re-transcribing audio through the old API isn't necessary. UtteranceEdit already tracks every modification, so I can pull Gladia's original outputs from beforeText and use those directly as the baseline, free, and perfectly aligned.

### Test split: most recent meetings across municipalities, held out to prevent speaker leakage. I plan to use three metrics:

* ### Normalized WER/CER: after Greek NFKC normalization (accent marks kept, ASCII punctuation stripped)

* ### Domain-Specific WER (DS-WER): isolated accuracy on municipal terminology, it applies the standard Levenshtein distance formula (Substitutions \+ Deletions \+ Insertions / N) exclusively to a predefined subset of domain-critical words: council member names, and local acronyms, which is where most of the meaningful errors actually live.

* Human Intervention Rate (HIR) Reduction. The editedBy flag in UtteranceEdit already tracks whether a correction came from the LLM (task) or a human reviewer (user), which gives us a real operational baseline to work from. Lowering theoretical WER matters, but the actual target is reducing how often a human has to touch a transcript.

**Validation strategy**

To catch overfitting during training, I'll hold out 2 municipalities entirely, one large and one small (say, Χαλάνδρι and Σαμοθράκη). These never appear in training and cover different acoustic conditions and accents. The test set is the most recent meetings from the remaining municipalities, split by date, so speakers from training don't leak into the evaluation.

**Concrete target**

Going by published results from Whisper fine tuning on similar low-resource domains, I'm aiming for 15-20% relative WER reduction on domain-specific terms (municipal vocabulary, proper nouns) and a measurable drop in Human Intervention Rate. If DS-WER improves but HIR doesn't, the fine tuning isn't fixing what actually matters.

### Production Integration & Inference Speed

### The fine tuned model gets merged with the base weights and converted to CTranslate2, then served through faster-whisper for roughly 3-4x speedup over HuggingFace Transformers inference. On a single GPU the goal is a real-time factor under 0.5x, so a 4 hour council meeting finishes transcription in under 2 hours. WhisperTranscriber drops in where GladiaTranscriber currently sits in transcribe.ts, same interface, nothing else in the pipeline changes.

### Risks and Mitigations

**1\. Insufficient training data.** 335 hours is moderate for ASR fine tuning. If LoRA doesn't converge well, I'll mix in HParl (Greek Parliament speech) and Common Voice Greek, which overlap enough to be useful. Mixing ratios get tuned on the validation set.

**2\. Overfitting to dominant municipalities.** Χανιά and Αθήνα make up over half the data. I'll use weighted sampling so smaller municipalities aren't drowned out, and the held out municipality split should catch generalization failures early.

**3\. Inference speed regression.** If CTranslate2 conversion loses quality, I'll benchmark against [ONNX Runtime](https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime) as a fallback. Speed gets validated before anything goes into production.

### 

![][image2]

Figure 3: Proposed Project Plan Overview

**How will it impact Open Technologies Alliance (GFOSS)?**  
Every minute saved is capacity the team gets back. OpenCouncil already works with over ten municipalities, and as new ones join, the recording backlog grows fast. A four-hour council meeting currently takes around two hours to review and confirm. Getting that under one hour per meeting is the concrete target. At half the effort, the system can grow without the human workload becoming the ceiling. Keeping public records current becomes more realistic as coverage spreads.

A 4 hour council meeting takes about 2 hours of human review right now. The goal is to cut that in half by producing a cleaner first pass transcript. With 10+ partner municipalities already and more coming on, review time is the bottleneck that decides how fast OpenCouncil can actually grow. The fine tuned model and training pipeline will be open-sourced, so other GFOSS projects or anyone doing Greek NLP work can use it as a starting point for civic speech recognition.

## Timeline (12 Weeks / 350 Hours)

**Community bonding (May 4 \- June 1\)**

* Build the data extraction pipeline: correction pairs from UtteranceEdit to a HuggingFace dataset  
* Write a Greek-aware WER/CER evaluation script (NFKC normalization, punctuation handling)  
* Set up GPU environment, verify training dependencies  
* Agree on a weekly sync schedule with mentors

**Communication:** Weekly 30 minute sync with mentors, either on video or as a written async update. Progress tracked through GitHub issues and PRs on the OpenCouncil repo. Training experiments logged in Weights & Biases so mentors can see what's happening.

**Phase 1: data and baselines (weeks 1-5)**

* Weeks 1-3: dataset work. Classify errors automatically (acoustic vs. linguistic) to decide what goes to ASR fine tuning vs. prompt extraction. Preprocess audio (noise reduction, VAD). Concatenate utterances into 2-5 min training segments. Split train/validation/test using 2 held out municipalities plus a date-based test split.  
* Weeks 4-5: baseline evaluation. Get Gladia baseline WER/CER from UtteranceEdit.beforeText. I'll compare against four baselines: Gladia's current output (reconstructed from UtteranceEdit.beforeText), zero shot Whisper-large-v3, the best community fine tune out there, Charalampos/whisper-medium-el (11.43% WER on Common Voice), and the [new Cohere transcribe model](https://www.google.com/url?source=gmail&sa=E&q=https://huggingface.co/CohereLabs/cohere-transcribe-03-2026).  
* **Milestone 1**: reproducible dataset on HuggingFace \+ baseline performance report.

**Phase 2: fine tuning (weeks 6-9)**

* Weeks 6-7: LoRA training with a hyperparameter sweep (rank, alpha, learning rate). Evaluate on held-out municipalities after each run.  
* Weeks 8-9: go after morphological errors and domain terminology specifically. Run ablation studies (with/without concatenation, different data subsets).  
* **Milestone 2**: fine tuned LoRA adapter with 15%+ relative DS-WER improvement over the Gladia baseline.

**Phase 3: production and documentation (weeks 10-12)**

* Week 10: convert to CTranslate2, benchmark inference speed (targeting a real-time factor under 0.5x).  
* Week 11: plug WhisperTranscriber into the production pipeline, run end-to-end tests on new meetings.  
* Week 12: write documentation, reproducibility scripts, and the final evaluation report.  
* **Final milestone**: production-ready two stage pipeline merged into the OpenCouncil repo.

**Commitment**

30 hours per week, no competing commitments for the full program duration.

**Post-GSoC**

I plan to stay on as a maintainer of the transcription module and keep contributing to the broader project.

### Stretch Goals (time permitting)

*Confidence-based LLM routing and [n-best lists](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.09554).* Switch inference to beam search (`num_beams=5`) and derive per-utterance confidence from it. High-confidence segments skip the LLM correction pass entirely. Utilize methods like [Word Confusion Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11640) for token efficiency.

[*Dynamic vocabulary injection*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.17410)*.* Build per-municipality glossaries from three sources: static DB entities (council members, party names), agenda-parsed NER (session-specific names, ΑΔΑ codes), and TF-IDF keywords from past transcripts. Inject these into the LLM prompt to stop it from hallucinating domain terms.

[*Few-shot prompt optimization*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165)*.* Take the most frequent error types from the correction dataset and use them as few-shot examples, paired with the dynamic vocabulary.

On the experimental side: I want to try agent-driven hyperparameter sweeps (the "[AutoResearch](https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch)" pattern) to run LoRA tuning cycles overnight without babysitting them.

Alternative architecture: If inference speed with Whisper \+ CTranslate2 isn't enough, [wav2vec2-BERT](https://huggingface.co/papers/2312.05187) (Meta, 2024, 580M params) does non-autoregressive decoding at 10-30x faster and is worth trying as a deployment option.

## Future extension: open-source LLM and RLHF

### In the near future, it is possible to move the error correction stage off commercial APIs and onto a locally hosted open-source model (Llama 3 or Mistral) that can also be fine tuned. The prompting and vocabulary injection methods carry over directly. The main gains are no recurring API costs and full data privacy.

### The bigger opportunity is continuous learning. Every time an OpenCouncil reviewer accepts, rejects, or edits a suggested correction, that action is implicitly a preference signal. Collected over time, those interactions form a training set for [Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3743127), teaching the model to correct more accurately on Greek civic speech, and specifically to avoid the over-correction problem that general-purpose LLMs tend to have on domain-specific text.

### 

### Bibliographical Research:

* **Whisper Fine-Tuning on Non-English**: Timmel et al. (2024), [*Fine-tuning Whisper on Low-Resource Languages for Real-World Applications*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.15726) demonstrates that fine-tuned models can outperform the base v3 on certain languages and explicitly addresses the long-form segmentation degradation issue that we will mitigate.  
* **LLM Post-Correction (Zero-Shot)**: [*Can Generative Large Language Models Perform ASR Error Correction?*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.04172) (2023) demonstrates that generative LLMs reduce WER across multiple ASR systems and datasets in zero/few-shot settings, without any retraining.  
* **LLM Post-Correction (Domain Adaptation)**: [*ASR Error Correction using Large Language Models*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.09554) (2024) emphasizes that error correction is critical for domain adaptation in black-box ASR systems, and proposes N-best list strategies to reduce hallucination risks.  
* **Rare Word / Personalized Vocabulary**: [*LLM-based Generative Error Correction for Rare Words with Synthetic Data and Phonetic Context*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.17410) (Yamashita et al., 2025\)  shows that incorporating domain-specific/rare words through synthetic data and phonetic context significantly improves rare-word correction and reduces overall error rates.  
* **LM-Enhanced Whisper**: de Zuazo et al. (2025), [*Whisper-LM: Improving ASR Models with Language Models for Low-Resource Languages*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.23542) integrates language models with fine-tuned Whisper, achieving up to 51% WER reduction on low-resource datasets.  
* **Word Confusion Network**: Liu (2020), [Jointly Encoding Word Confusion Network and Dialogue Context with BERT for Spoken Language Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11640)  
* **Few-Shot Prompting:** Brown, Tom B., et al. (2020) ["Language Models are Few-Shot Learners". Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 33](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165).   
* **The Greek Podcast corpus**: Paraskevopoulous, et al. (2024), [Competitive speech models for low-resourced languages with weakly supervised data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.15284)

## Appendix A: Corrections’ sample analysis and error taxonomy

### A.1 Dataset overview

The correction dataset contains 4,862 aligned pairs of ASR output (`before_text`) and human-corrected transcription (`after_text`), pulled from live municipal council sessions across several Greek municipalities. Each pair includes utterance timestamps and a URL pointing to the source audio on opencouncil.gr, so you can use it directly for supervised fine-tuning without extra preprocessing.

The recordings come from actual council chambers: reverberant rooms, people talking over each other, microphones that aren't always in the right place, and speakers drifting between formal and informal registers. Legal language, administrative jargon, proper nouns, and acronyms show up constantly.

### A.2 Analysis methodology

Two independent analyses were run on the dataset, combining automated classification with manual review.

**Step 1: Rule-based pre-classification.** Before any diff analysis, simple rules filtered out surface-level differences: if stripping punctuation made the two texts identical, the pair got classified as punctuation-only. `.lower()` caught capitalization-only cases. Whitespace normalization caught formatting artifacts.

**Step 2: Word-level diff.** For the remaining pairs, `difflib.SequenceMatcher` identified word-level changes between before and after. Each change received a character-level similarity score (0-1).

**Step 3: Threshold categorization.** Similarity above 0.7 was tagged morphological/phonetic. Between 0.4 and 0.7, likely phonetic confusion. Below 0.4, a significant semantic substitution. Words starting with a capital letter and below a length threshold were flagged as potential proper nouns.

**Step 4: Manual review.** Several hundred pairs were reviewed by hand to validate the automated classifications and catch patterns that the thresholds missed.

**Known limitations.** The capital-letter heuristic for proper nouns also catches sentence-initial words. The phonetic/morphological distinction relies on text similarity, not acoustic analysis, so some ASR errors score high on character overlap and get misclassified as morphological. Without access to the audio, there's no way to tell whether the speaker misspoke or the ASR misrecognized correct speech. The two analyses produced slightly different breakdowns. That's inherent ambiguity in error categorization, not analytical error.

### A.3 Error taxonomy

Errors fall into three categories based on which pipeline stage is best positioned to fix them.

**Category A: ASR-correctable errors (\~42%, \~2,030 cases)**

These happen at the acoustic recognition stage. Fine-tuning Whisper on domain-specific audio-text pairs is the primary fix.

*Phonetic confusions (\~19%, \~918 cases).* The ASR recognized a word that sounded like the correct one but wasn't. Many involve phonological features specific to Greek:

| ASR Output | Correct | Error type |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| μεσαπαρακάτα | μερικά παρακάτω | Word boundary |
| τους τολήνουμε | το λύνουμε | Cliticization |
| στείγει | θίγει | Consonant confusion (στ/θ) |
| πτέσματα | πταίσματα | Iotacism (ε/αι) |
| χτήματα | κτήματα | Cluster confusion (χτ/κτ) |

Greek iotacism, where /i/ can be spelled ι, η, ει, οι, or υι, causes recurring confusion. So do consonant clusters that don't appear in most of Whisper's training languages.

*Proper noun errors (\~11%, \~552 cases).* This is the single biggest quality problem in municipal council transcription. Greek surnames aren't in Whisper's vocabulary, and each municipality brings its own council member names, street names, and local organizations.

| ASR Output | Correct | Context |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Λουτσία | Λουτσέτη | Council member |
| Ρσένια | Αρσένη | Council member |
| Μπακογιάρη | Μπακογιάννη | Public figure |
| Κασιλόριζο | Καστελόριζο | Place name |

Fine-tuning helps for names it has seen in training data. It struggles to adapt to unfamiliar names. This is precisely why the LLM's post-processing layer requires a vocabulary that can change depending on the specific municipality. Fine-tuning the model on its own isn't enough to address this issue.

*Important content substitutions (\~8%, \~406 cases).* The ASR produced a completely different phrase: "ίδιοι" recognized as "η ΔΕΗ," "σωματεία" as "ομάδες." These likely come from noise or overlapping speech. The acoustic distance between output and ground truth is too large for fine-tuning to bridge reliably. Better audio preprocessing (noise reduction, VAD) would help more.

*Missing or hallucinated speech (\~3%, \~154 cases).* 75 cases where ASR generated text over silence (hallucination), 64 cases of truncated transcription, 15 cases where speech just wasn't recognized. VAD-based chunking in faster-whisper directly addresses the hallucination cases.

**Category B: LLM-correctable errors (\~25%, \~1,211 cases)**

These have nothing to do with acoustics. They're formatting and grammar issues that Whisper produces because it wasn't designed to handle them. An LLM post-processing layer can correct them with high accuracy.

*Punctuation and capitalization (\~22%, \~1,048 cases).* The largest single subcategory. Missing commas (\~457), missing periods (\~488), wrong sentence boundaries (\~175), and capitalization errors. One detail worth noting: the Greek question mark (ερωτηματικό `;`) is a different Unicode character from the Latin semicolon, but Whisper's tokenizer defaults to ASCII. 14 instances of this specific error were found. A single normalization rule applied before the LLM stage catches all of them.

*Whitespace and formatting (\~2%, \~104 cases).* Double spaces and similar artifacts. A regex pass before the LLM stage handles these cheaply.

*Article/gender agreement (\~1%, \~45 cases).* Greek grammatical agreement errors: "τις παρόντες" corrected to "τους παρόντες." An LLM with Greek language understanding resolves these from the syntactic context.

Domain-specific patterns (\~3%, \~225 cases). Administrative codes (51 cases), numeric corrections like date errors (97 cases), and unintelligible section markers (77 cases). The acronym errors are typical: "ΣΥΔΟΜ" instead of "ΥΔΟΜ," "ΑΤΑΔ" instead of "ΕΤΑΔ," "ΚΕΦΗ" instead of "ΚΗΦΗ." Give the LLM a list of valid acronyms per municipality, and it fixes nearly all of these.

**Category C: Overlap zone (\~33%, \~1,621 cases)**

*Morphological errors (\~27%, \~1,296 cases).* The most common ambiguous category. The errors affect root morphemes, not suffixes:

| ASR Output | Correct | Error |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| αρχιοθετείτε | αρχειοθετείται | αρχιο- vs αρχειο- |
| καταδαφιστεί | κατεδαφιστεί | καταδαφ- vs κατεδαφ- |
| απαλωτρίωση | απαλλοτρίωση | απαλωτρ- vs απαλλοτρ- |
| γυροκομείο | γηροκομείο | γυρο- vs γηρο- |

Root errors strongly suggest ASR mistakes rather than speaker errors. Speakers generally pronounce these correctly, but Whisper's language model biases toward more frequent (wrong) forms. The recommended approach: target this category primarily through fine-tuning, with the LLM as a fallback for cases the fine-tuned model still misses.

*Mixed errors (\~7%, \~325 cases).* Utterances with multiple simultaneous error types, like a phonetic confusion and a punctuation error in the same sentence. These need both pipeline stages working in sequence.

### A.4 High-frequency substitutions

Some substitutions recur across many sessions:

| ASR Output | Correct | Frequency |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| δικιά | δική | 6 |
| παρόν | παρών | 5 |
| συσπήρωση | συσπείρωση | 5 |
| πίστοσης | πίστωσης | 4 |
| απαλωτρίωση | απαλλοτρίωση | 4 |

A fine-tuned model that gets these specific words right will have an outsized effect on overall quality, since the errors compound across sessions.

### A.5 Domain vocabulary reference

Municipal council proceedings use a recognizable set of administrative terms, acronyms, and place names. These appear frequently in the error data and should form the core of both the fine-tuning vocabulary and the LLM's dynamic term lists.

**Organizations:** ΥΔΟΜ, ΔΕΗ, ΚΤΕΛ, ΔΗ.Κ.Ε.Π.Α.Ο, ΔΕΥΑΑΡΜ, ΕΔΑΕΜ, ΚτΥΠ

**Legal and administrative terms:** απαλλοτρίωση, αρχειοθετείται, ψηφοφορία, εισήγηση, απόφαση

**Place names:** Ξυλόκαστρο, Ευρωστίνη, Καστελόριζο, Ηράκλειο, Χανιά
